title: Abdominal oesophagus
body_html:
The oesophagus has a short abdominal section. The abdominal oesophagus blends smoothly with the gastric mucosa. The abdominal oesophagus commences at the diaphragmatic opening and terminates at the gastro-oesophogeal junction (GOJ). For the exam you need to know the anatomical level these relate to and important anatomical relations:

Abdominal oesophagus anatomical levels
  • Diaphragm opening at T10 vertebral level OR 7th costal cartilage level
  • Gastric cardia/GOJ at T11 vetebral level

Anatomical relations
StructurePosition in relation to oesophagus
Peritoneum
Left vagus nerve
Posterior surface of heart
Left lobe of liver		Anterior
Right vagus nerve
Left crus of diaphragm		Posterior

Factors guarding against reflux
The GOJ acts like a sphincter and is sometimes referred to as the lower oesophageal sphincter. It is a physiological sphincter rather than an anatomical one. It is classified like this as it does not have sphincteric muscle. The physiological sphincter is formed from:
  • Fibres of right crus of diaphragm 'pinch' or have a noose like effect on the oesophagus.
  • The folds of muscularis mucosa form flaps which help occlude the oesophageal lumen and prevent reflux
  • Angulation of the oesophagus as the oesophagus enters the stomach at an acute angle.

Image sourced from Wikipedia
Diagram illustrating the upper and thoracic sections of the oesophagus

Inferior view of oesophagus passing through the diagram. Note how the left crus passes posterior to the oesophagus
